source_intake_record.raw_question_text: As shown in the figure, Master Zhao uses a piece of triangular tinplate to cut out a piece of square tinplate for reserve. In $\triangle ABC$, $BC=120$, the height $AD=80$, the square $EFGH$ has its side $GH$ on side $BC$, and $E, F$ are respectively on sides $AB, AC$. Then, what is the length of the side of the square $EFGH$?
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text:
\textbf{Solution:} Set the side length of the square part as $x$.\\
In square $EFGH$, $EF\parallel BC$, $EH\parallel AD$\\
$\therefore \angle AEF=\angle ABC$, $\angle EAF=\angle BAC$; $\angle BHE=\angle BDA$, $\angle B=\angle B$\\
$\therefore \triangle AEF\sim \triangle ABC$, $\triangle BEH\sim \triangle BAD$\\
$\therefore \frac{EF}{BC}=\frac{AE}{AB}$, $\frac{EH}{AD}=\frac{BE}{BA}$\\
$\therefore \frac{EF}{BC}+\frac{EH}{AD}=\frac{AE}{AB}+\frac{BE}{BA}=1$\\
$\therefore \frac{x}{120}+\frac{x}{80}=1$ \\
Solving for $x$ gives: $x=\boxed{48}$

requires_image=true and text_dominant=false both indicate meaningful visual reliance
image_manifest has 1 registered image and registry_passed=true, so the visual asset is expected to be part of the sample

the answer_text explicitly uses multiple linked steps: parallel-line angle matching, two similarity statements, ratio combination, and algebra

verifiability: target answer is a single numeric side length, so correctness is easy to check against the derived equation
answer_text provides an explicit derivation ending with x/120 + x/80 = 1 and x = 48, which is rule-checkable
question and answer are both present and asset_registry_record.registry_passed=true, reducing ambiguity in downstream verification
